So if you want Codex to work really well for you, I would say there's four things in this framework. One, just pick something repetitive, right? Start with a workflow that you already know well, something that's easy to verify and easy to judge. Don't begin with the hardest, most ambitious thing you can imagine, although for some go for it. Um, if you're, if you have confidence with that. But start with the real workflow perfectly. One that's a little bit annoying for you sometimes happens over and over again. Second, is just give Codex the right materials, put in the files, you know, docs, c, SV notes, what have you into the workspace that you give it access to. A big part of the success here is simply giving Codex enough context, right? To do the job well. And so this is also one of the reasons Codex is so useful when work actually lives in your local computer folder and files. And the third is just describe the outcome. Clearly I put in a simple prompting technique here, but be specific with your, with your goal, what good looks like, any examples or what to do, what not to do, and how Codex will know when it's done. You, you don't need to tell it every single technical step like I showed earlier, but ideally you can define the task just clearly enough so it knows what success means. And then the last is just iterate until it works, right? Maybe the first time you asked Codex to do something, it didn't do it correctly. Um, the first result is often the starting point, really not the final answer. So just review what Codex did. Maybe ask Codex to explain its logic, point out what it did wrong.
